quick_summary: REST is the dominant architectural style for web APIs, used by virtually every major platform. Interviewers probe your understanding of its constraints, HTTP method semantics, status codes, and how to design clean, predictable API endpoints.

detailed_content.explanation:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style, not a protocol. It defines six constraints: client-server separation, statelessness, cacheability, uniform interface, layered system, and optional code-on-demand. The uniform interface is the key — resources are identified by URIs, manipulated through representations (JSON/XML), and operations use standard HTTP methods.

Think of REST like a well-organized library: every book (resource) has a unique location (URI), you can read it (GET), replace it (PUT), update parts (PATCH), or remove it (DELETE). The librarian (server) doesn't remember your previous visits (stateless) — you bring your library card (token) each time.
detailed_content.key_takeaway: REST maps CRUD operations to HTTP methods (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) on resource URIs — keep URLs noun-based and methods verb-based.
detailed_content.try_it: Design RESTful endpoints for a blog platform with posts, comments, and users. What HTTP methods and status codes would each operation use?

pro_tip: In interviews, always discuss proper HTTP status codes (201 Created, 204 No Content, 404 Not Found, 422 Unprocessable Entity), HATEOAS for discoverability, versioning strategies (URL vs header), and pagination patterns (cursor-based vs offset-based). Mention Richardson Maturity Model to show depth.
